content.full_text:
State of California 
From 
mol'" 
m 
: The File 
fAIR PO UTI CAL PRA 
John Giansello 
ISSION 
Opinion Request of CSEA dated March 14, 1980 
Dote 
March 28, 1980 
Called Bernard Allemano at CSEA and informed him that our record-
keeping regulation, 2 Cal. Admin. Code §18401, would govern the item-
ization problem they presented. 
CSEA need not keep records of dues 
transactions involving contributions received per member of less than 
$25.00, other than the date and total amount of such contributions. 
The cumulative total of such transactions of less than $25.00 per 
member will not produce any itemization obligation for CSEA. 
With respect to contributions solicited and received directly 
from members, itemization will be required when the total of separate 
contributions of $25.00 or more received from any particular member 
reaches $100. 
Regarding the question raised about 2 Cal. Admin. Code §l8419, 
"member contribution" as used in §18419(a) (5) and §184l9(d) means any 
contribution earmarked for use by the sponsoring organization's 
political action committee. 
If an intermediate unit as defined in 
§18419(a} (3) is a committee by reason of contributions received and 
expenditures made independently of the sponsoring organization or the 
latter's political action committee, then the intermediate unit will 
be required to itemize all contributions received and expenditures 
of $100 or more with respect to which it acts independently. 
Contributions earmarked for use of the sponsoring organization or 
its political action committee ("member contributions") will be 
reported and itemized only by the sponsoring organization's political 
action committee, not by the intermediate unit, in accordance with 
§18419 (d) . 


State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
: Opinion Request Meeting Participants 
From 
: 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTlCt~/lJl_ISSION 
John Giansello ~J~ 
Subject: 
Date 
March 14, 1980 
The California State Employees Association is a sponsoring orga-
nization within the meaning of 2 Cal. Admin. Code §184l9. 
Members pay dues to CSEA. 
Fifty cents per member is earmarked in 
advance for political purposes and transferred by CSEA to its political 
action committee. 
In some years this earmarking produces a surplus for 
the political action committee which it carries over for political 
purposes in succeeding years. 
A certain portion of the dues money not allocated for political 
purposes is rebated to CSEA's local chapters for general, non-political 
purposes. 
CSEA's political action committee now proposes the following: 
(1) To solicit members directly for contributions to the 
political action committee. 
(2) To ask the local chapters to contribute a portion of 
rebated funds to the political action committee. 
In addition, the CSEA board in 1979 voted to authorize the turn-
over of $84,000 of non-earmarked 1979 funds to the political action 
committee for the purpose of opposing Proposition 9. 
The transaction 
authorized by this action was not accomplished until 1980. 
In a 
previous year (1978) the political action committee had returned 
$75,000 of earmarked funds to the CSEA general fund for use for non-
political purposes. 
(A) Assuming that CSEA, its local chapters and its political 
action committee report in accordance with 2 Cal. Admin. Code §184l9, 
which of the above actions must be taken into consideration to 
determine which contributors must be itemized on the political action 
committee's reports? 
(B) If the amount of rebated, non-earmarked funds is to be 
considered, is it sufficient to apportion the amount rebated from a 
particular chapter against the number of members of that chapter? 
(The total amount of dues paid CSEA by each member is determined by 
his or her income. 
Therefore, the portion of rebated funds actually 
attributable to each member will vary from one member to another.) 


Opinion Request Meeting Participants 
-2-
March 14, 1980 
(C) If the amount of the CSEA board turnover is to be considered 
-
on the grounds that there is a sufficient history to indicate to the 
membership that the 50¢ per month per member earmarking is not neces-
sarily all of the dues money that will be spent for political purposes 
in a particular year - is it sufficient to apportion the total amount 
of the turnover against the total membership of CSEA? 
(The same 
concern about variable dues payments as expressed in (B) applies here.) 
(D) If the reasonable expectations of the membership are affected 
by the $84,000 turnover of non-political funds for political purposes, 
what affect if any does the return in a prior year of a carry-over 
political surplus to the non-political fund have? 
(E) What affect if any on these itemization problems does our 
recordkeeping regulation, 2 Cal. Admin. Code §18401, have when it says 
that the accounts required to be kept by treasurers need not contain 
detail as to contributions received of less than $25 other than the 
date and total amount of such contributions? 
If, in addition to all of the other activity described above, a 
local CSEA Chapter raises $500 in additional contributions directly 
from its members to be spent directly by the local chapter so that by 
virtue of activities independent of the sponsoring organization the 
local chapter itself becomes a recipient committee, does 2 Cal. Admin. 
Code §18419(d) mean that the local chapter need not report these con-
tributions regardless of amount? 
Note that by virtue of its role in 
the CSEA scheme the local chapter is an "intermediate unit." 
18419(d) 
seems to imply that such an intermediate unit may also become a com-
mittee by reason of "other political activities." 
Because it is an 
intermediate unit whose name is not substantially different from that 
of the sponsoring organization, it need not be listed as an inter-
mediary on the parent's political action committee reports by 
18419(b) (4). 
Furthermore, with respect to contributions raised and 
spent directly by the local chapter, the latter is not in fact an 
intermediary. 
Yet 18419(d) says that an intermediate unit which is a 
committee by virtue of other political activities need not disclose 
"member contributions" if its PAC follows the procedures set forth in 
18419(b) (4) (which here produce no reporting). 
Is the answer that the restriction of "member contribution" by 
18419(a) (5) to payments by members of the sponsoring organization or 
intermediate unit earmarked for use by the sponsor's political action 
committee together with the nondisclosure language of 18419{d) means 
funds raised and spent by an intermediate unit independently of its 
parent or the parent's political action committee are governed by the 
same rules applicable to other committees. 
In other words, if the 
intermediate unit is a committee by virtue of its activity conducted 
independently of the parent and the parent's political action com-
mittee, the intermediate unit must itemize all contributions and 
expenditures of $100 or more with respect to which it acts indepen-
dently? 


r~arch 5, 1980 
State cf California 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
P. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95804 
Attention: 
Mr. Dwight Dickerson: 
Dear Mr. Dickerson: 
I would appreciate an opinion from you regarding any potential conflict of 
interest I may have as it would pertain to voting on issues relating to 
rent control or mandatory binding arbitration legislation enacted by our 
City Council. 
I am currently an owner of apartment units scattered throughout the City 
of Burbank. 
It is my belief that the voting against rent control is not 
a conflict of interest, and the City Attorney has not suggested to me that 
such a conflict is present. 
Hcwever, I would appreciate your opinion as I 
am sure you have had to rule on this matter many times before. 
I hope this 
will not cause you greater inconvenience. 
Also, I would further state that all members of the City Council are land-
lords and rent property within the City of Burbank and we have, as a Council ~ 
opposed rent control as we feel it is counter-productive to the free enter-
prise system and the results of it would be to eliminate new apartmert 
buildings from being developed in Burbank. 
No individual has suggested that 
our position could be considered as a conflict of interest but, nevertheless, 
I feel it is certainly in my best interest to have an opinion from your office. 
Thank you in advance. 
Best regards, 
da 
Ri chman 
i lman

sections.question: Multiple questions regarding itemization requirements for CSEA political contributions under 2 Cal. Admin. Code §18419: (A) Which actions must be considered to determine which contributors must be itemized? (B) Is apportionment by chapter membership sufficient for rebated funds? (C) Is apportionment by total membership sufficient for board turnovers? (D) What effect does return of prior year surplus have? (E) What effect does the recordkeeping regulation §18401 have on itemization? (F) Must local chapters report independently raised contributions if they become recipient committees?
sections.conclusion: CSEA need not keep records of dues transactions involving contributions received per member of less than $25.00, other than the date and total amount. Itemization is required when total separate contributions of $25.00 or more from any particular member reaches $100. 'Member contribution' means contributions earmarked for the sponsoring organization's political action committee. Intermediate units acting independently must itemize all contributions and expenditures of $100 or more with respect to which they act independently; member contributions are reported only by the sponsoring organization's political action committee.
sections.question_synthetic: CSEA seeks guidance on itemization requirements for its political action committee when members make direct contributions, local chapters contribute rebated funds, and the board authorizes transfers of non-earmarked funds to the PAC, including how to allocate these contributions to individual members for reporting purposes.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC advised that itemization thresholds apply at the individual member level ($100 total from any member triggers itemization), with the $25 recordkeeping threshold allowing simplified records. 'Member contributions' are limited to amounts earmarked for the PAC and reported by it, while contributions from intermediate units acting independently are governed by standard committee rules requiring itemization of $100+ contributions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple parts: memorandum with detailed questions, FPPC's substantive response, and an unrelated incoming request from Richard Richman regarding rent control conflict of interest. Extraction focuses on the CSEA advice letter portion (80A097). Confidence reduced due to OCR quality issues in original text and multiple sections combined in single document ID.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Multiple questions regarding itemization requirements for CSEA political contributions under 2 Cal. Admin. Code §18419: (A) Which actions must be considered to determine which contributors must be itemized? (B) Is apportionment by chapter membership sufficient for rebated funds? (C) Is apportionment by total membership sufficient for board turnovers? (D) What effect does return of prior year surplus have? (E) What effect does the recordkeeping regulation §18401 have on itemization? (F) Must local chapters report independently raised contributions if they become recipient committees?

CONCLUSION: CSEA need not keep records of dues transactions involving contributions received per member of less than $25.00, other than the date and total amount. Itemization is required when total separate contributions of $25.00 or more from any particular member reaches $100. 'Member contribution' means contributions earmarked for the sponsoring organization's political action committee. Intermediate units acting independently must itemize all contributions and expenditures of $100 or more with respect to which they act independently; member contributions are reported only by the sponsoring organization's political action committee.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to CSEA's opinion request regarding itemization obligations for its political action committee under §18419, addressing dues transfers, direct member solicitations, local chapter contributions, and board-authorized fund transfers. The advice clarifies that member-level contribution thresholds and the definition of 'member contributions' determine itemization requirements.
